serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side dish serving of stir-fried green beans with garlic is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a small bowl or side plate portion as commonly served in Singaporean hawker centres and Vietnamese home cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Green Beans With Garlic (Dau Que Xao Toi) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Stir Fried Green Beans With Garlic (Dau Que Xao Toi), cr_id G00011036. All per-100g values are sourced from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Green beans (cooked) — Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load values from the International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried green beans with garlic as a composite dish. GI of 32 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked green beans (GI ≈ 32, low category). The addition of garlic and small amounts of sauce does not materially alter the GI of the vegetable base. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (32 × 22.86 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 10.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 314 mg/100 g from oyster/fish sauce with insufficient potassium offset (156 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or less and request reduced oyster/fish sauce preparation, as sodium at 314 mg/100 g with potassium of only 156 mg/100 g provides an unfavourable Na:K ratio; substituting low-sodium soy sauce and increasing garlic quantity would meaningfully reduce sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 22.86 g/100 g with low dietary fibre (2.0 g/100 g) and high fat (12.86 g/100 g) from added oil
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should be cautious with this dish due to a carbohydrate-to-fibre ratio of approximately 11:1, which limits glycaemic buffering; requesting preparation with minimal oil and reduced oyster sauce would lower both caloric density and added sugars, and a 100 g portion contributes approximately 7.3 g glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 12.86 g/100 g with estimated saturated fat 1.8 g/100 g and sodium 314 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals managing cardiovascular disease, the relatively high total fat content (12.86 g/100 g) and sodium (314 mg/100 g) from added oil and sauce warrant portion control; however, green beans provide dietary fibre (2.0 g/100 g) and garlic contributes allicin with cardioprotective anti-inflammatory properties, making a reduced-oil, reduced-sauce version a more heart-friendly option.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory garlic polyphenols (allicin) and vitamin C from green beans; low saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as green beans provide antioxidant vitamin C and garlic contributes anti-inflammatory allicin; however, sodium at 314 mg/100 g may worsen tissue swelling, so requesting reduced sauce and less oil is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Green Beans with Garlic (Dau Que Xao Toi) is a Vietnamese-origin vegetable side dish widely served in Singaporean hawker centres and home kitchens. The dish consists of fresh green beans (French beans or long beans) rapidly stir-fried over high heat with minced or sliced garlic in cooking oil, typically seasoned with oyster sauce and/or fish sauce to add umami depth and a slight savoury sweetness. The cooking technique produces tender-crisp beans with lightly caramelised garlic and a glossy sauce coating. Per 100 g, the dish provides 223 kcal, 4.04 g protein, 12.86 g fat, and 22.86 g carbohydrate, with 2.0 g dietary fibre and 314 mg sodium. The relatively high fat content reflects the use of added cooking oil, and the sodium level is driven by oyster or fish sauce. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension and type 2 diabetes due to sodium and carbohydrate load relative to fibre, but the garlic component provides allicin with anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective properties. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for most dietary patterns when prepared with reduced sauce and oil. It is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner side dish in Singapore and Vietnam.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for this exact dish (cr_id G00011036) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The HPB is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean food records. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest analogous food (cooked green beans, GI 32) as no direct composite GI measurement exists.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this stir-fried composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00011036) as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this dish. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data beyond the reported minerals (potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron) and cholesterol are not available in the HPB source for this dish as of 2026. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero trans fat for a vegetable stir-fry prepared with standard cooking oil per HPB and USDA FoodData Central reference data for comparable dishes. saturated_fat_g of 1.8 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable stir-fried vegetable dishes with similar total fat content, as the HPB source does not disaggregate saturated fat; this is documented as an adapted value. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on cooked green beans from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists in approved sources. The dish name includes a Vietnamese component (Dau Que Xao Toi) reflecting its Vietnamese culinary origin as served in Singapore; multilingual coverage includes Vietnamese, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish requirements.
